q: She packed her umbrella. She would not get wet in case it started raining. (Join the sentences, using 'so that'.) 2024
a: She packed her umbrella so that she would not get wet in case it started raining.

q: My mother has been on a diet. She wants to be slim. (Join the sentences, using 'so that'.) 2024
a: My mother has been on a diet so that she can be slim.

q: My aunt is a brilliant woman. Everyone admires her. (Join the sentences, using 'so ... that' / 'such ... that'.) 2024
a: My aunt is so brilliant that everyone admires her. (OR) My aunt is such a brilliant woman that everyone admires her.

q: Many students came to the farewell dinner. We couldn't get a seat. (Join the sentences, using 'so ... that' / 'such ... that'.) 2025
a: So many students came to the farewell dinner that we couldn't get a seat.

q: It was an amazing performance. The audience gave a standing ovation. (Join the sentences, using 'so...that'/'such...that'.) 2026
a: The performance was so amazing that the audience gave a standing ovation.

q: The girl has very good manners. Everyone likes her. (Join the sentences, using 'so...that'/'such...that'.) 2026
a: The girl has such good manners that everyone likes her.

q:
We went for a picnic yesterday. We hoped to have a relaxed atmosphere there.
(Join the sentences, using 'in order to'.) 2026
a: We went for a picnic yesterday in order to have a relaxed atmosphere there.

q:
The boy goes to the gym regularly. He wants to keep his body in shape.
(Join the sentences, using 'in order to'.) 2026
a: The boy goes to the gym regularly in order to keep his body in shape.
